title: Wake up — open the curtains
description: Light signals the brain that the day has started. Just this.
neuroscience: Morning light suppresses melatonin and anchors the circadian rhythm, stabilising sleep and mood cycles over time.

title: The relaxation sigh
description: One slow breath in through the nose. A long, quiet sigh out through the mouth.
neuroscience: An extended exhale activates the parasympathetic nervous system, slowing heart rate and reducing cortisol within seconds.

title: Relax your jaw and forehead
description: Notice where you are holding tension. Let it go.
neuroscience: Consciously releasing chronic muscle tension interrupts the stress feedback loop and signals safety to the nervous system.

title: Find one thing going right
description: Look for one small thing — anything. It does not have to be big.
neuroscience: The brain's negativity bias is a survival default. Consciously noting something positive begins to rebalance attention patterns over time.

title: Visit a happy memory
description: Close your eyes and go somewhere good. A place, a person, a moment. Stay there a while.
neuroscience: Recalling positive memories activates the same reward circuits as experiencing them, while building psychological distance from current difficulty.

title: Brush your teeth and wash your face
description: A small act of care for the person you are.
neuroscience: Basic hygiene tasks activate the reward system and restore a sense of agency, quietly resetting the brain's narrative around self-worth.

title: Step outside the door
description: Just outside. You do not have to go anywhere.
neuroscience: Even brief exposure to natural light raises serotonin and resets circadian cues. A change of environment alone shifts attention away from rumination.

title: Make one small decision
description: Choose something simple. What to eat, where to sit, which mug to use. Decide and do it.
neuroscience: Decision-making activates the prefrontal cortex, which is suppressed during low mood. Small decisions rebuild the neural pathways for agency and control.

title: Walk to a café and sit for a while
description: Order something. Sit. Let the world move around you.
neuroscience: Passive social contact — being in a social environment without pressure to interact — reduces cortisol and gently stimulates the social engagement system.

title: Finish one tiny chore
description: One thing. Make the bed, wipe the counter, take out one bag. Done.
neuroscience: Task completion releases dopamine. Small, achievable tasks rebuild the reward system when larger goals feel out of reach.

title: Go for a walk — no destination needed
description: Put on shoes and go. Anywhere. For as long as feels right.
neuroscience: Rhythmic movement synchronises the nervous system and raises BDNF, a protein that supports brain repair and mood regulation.

title: Text someone you trust
description: One message. You do not have to explain anything. Just reach out.
neuroscience: Social connection releases oxytocin and reduces the stress response. Even a brief exchange is enough to activate the bonding system.

title: Cook one simple meal
description: Something real to eat. As simple as you need it to be.
neuroscience: Food preparation engages sensory systems and provides a clear goal with a real outcome. Eating regularly stabilises blood sugar and mood regulation.

title: Sit somewhere with people nearby
description: A café, a park, a library. You do not have to speak to anyone.
neuroscience: Passive co-presence reduces the nervous system's threat response and gently activates the social engagement system without requiring interaction.

title: The 20-second hug
description: Find someone you trust. Hold for twenty seconds. Long enough for it to work.
neuroscience: Twenty seconds of physical contact is the threshold at which oxytocin release becomes significant, measurably reducing cortisol and creating calm.

title: Call someone — even for five minutes
description: Pick up the phone. Five minutes is enough.
neuroscience: Voice contact activates the vagal nerve and social engagement system more deeply than text. A brief call measurably lowers the physiological stress response.

title: Do something for someone else
description: Small is fine. Make a cup of tea, send a message, hold a door. The direction matters.
neuroscience: Prosocial behaviour activates the reward system and reduces self-focused rumination, shifting the brain from threat mode toward connection and meaning.

title: Write about something you look forward to
description: It does not have to be certain. Just something you can imagine.
neuroscience: Anticipating positive future events activates the same dopamine pathways as reward. Writing grounds the thought and makes the anticipated event feel more real.

title: Move your body hard for 20 minutes
description: Run, cycle, jump — anything that gets your heart going. All the way.
neuroscience: High-intensity movement triggers significant BDNF release and endorphin production, with measurable antidepressant effects lasting hours after the session.

title: Sit with your own thoughts for 5 minutes
description: No phone. No music. Just you. Let thoughts come and go without following them.
neuroscience: Brief mindfulness practice strengthens the prefrontal cortex's ability to regulate the amygdala, reducing emotional reactivity over time.
